title: First Block: Interview with Fabian Hedin, Co-Founder and CTO of Lovable

segments.text: It's actually a great time to create
startups and you know that's core of
like our business idea as well that hey
you don't actually need to know all of
these like technical skills that could
take you years to uh to acquire and
learn but you could just kind of have
this idea or or have this kind of
unrelated skill set to software and and
even do like a software startup.
Welcome to First Block, a Notion series
where founders from the world's leading
companies tell us what it was like to
navigate the many firsts of their
journey and what they learned from that
experience. Our guest today is Fabian
Hadin, co-founder and CTO of Lovable.
Welcome everyone to First Glock, where
we explore and dig into breakout
startups. And we have a doozy today. I
might say we have a lovable doozy. Uh my
name is Fuzzik Kad Shahi. I support the
engineering team at Notion as their CTO
and I'm really excited to be hosting and
talking with Fabian Hadin. Did I say
that right?
>> That's right.
>> Excellent. I won't make you say my last
name. It'll take forever. Um I I first
of all I want to welcome you and also
congratulate you on going from 1 million
to 100 million in like eight months. Uh,
I had a similar experience about 20
years ago where I went from one to 100,
but it was 1,000 in revenue to 100,000
revenue and I was thrilled. So, I can't
imagine how, you know, it's just
mindboggling going from 1 million to
100. [music] So, congratulations on
that.
I was wondering if you could take a step
back and before the hundred million,
before the million, before you became
lovable, um you I know that you coded
when you were in uh high school. I think
that's when you started, you had
multiple startups before that. Was it a
straight path and sort of like success
after success or was it like, you know,
what what was the journey like? What
were the events to leading up to meeting
with Anton and starting lovable?
>> I started coding a bit before high
school I guess. Um it was Minecraft
pulling me into it in in the beginning.
Uh so I was running this server and you
know you want to model it, you want to
change the experience and then you have
to create a website for it and you have
to figure out what is HTML, CSS and
JavaScript and uh you get into
programming that way at least for me. Um
and after that in high school I started
building um more kind of games uh on my
own and that quite quickly turned into
you know someone hearing like oh this
guy programs um I have this thing I want
to build let me let me ask him. So I I
started building actual like business
applications quite quite early in in
high school and uh that was more my
introduction to starting to build uh
like SAS products. Um, so I did that a
few times and then after a while I was
like this is a bit boring doing this for
like other people and their ideas and
and so on and I wanted to do something
uh that um I would be like a longerterm
stakeholder in and really do something
myself. So I teamed up with um one of my
my friends and and classmates and we um
right after high school on the summer we
uh started this kind of SAS real estate
startup and uh um yeah so that's the I I
guess my my first startup uh other than
the Minecraft server. Um after high
school I went to um I moved to Stockholm
the the biggest city the capital of of
Sweden and I went to the Royal Institute
of Technology um and there I started
meeting more like-minded people and um
one of those people actually introduced
me to to Anton
and um I had recently exited my my real
[clears throat] estate startup at that
point and uh it wasn't like this super
big exit But it was enough for me to not
be super worried about like, oh, I gotta
find a new job or whatever. And so I
wasn't really looking for anything, but
you know, it's always nice talking with
with people in the space. So I met with
Anon and his then co-founder and um I
ended up joining their startup which was
uh this YC startup called the Pix that
was based out in Stockholm. Uh so Anton
was my my boss. He was the CTO of that
startup. uh and I was there for 10
months and I'm proud to say that's my
longest employment uh to date. Um and um
after 10 months I I choose to quit um
because I I wanted to do my own thing
again. But then one day um Anton called
me up. I think it was a Saturday morning
and he was calling me waking me up and
like oh hey do you want to go take a
walk together? And I was like no not
really. [laughter]
Um but uh he's like oh I'm outside like
let's let's go for a walk and he asked
me do you know what GP engineer is so
this was the summer of 2023 uh and Anton
as a side project had created this kind
of CLI for creating software I didn't
know what it was [laughter] I was like
should I was a few days old at that
point so um I don't feel too bad for not
knowing it but we got to talking about
um um kind of um you know what what this
thing could uh could become and um you
know can we build a company around this
essentially and uh um you know it was
very clear to me and this is something
I've been thinking about for a while as
well of like oh hey what what can you do
with AI so this was like D34
territory and uh it was very clear that
like okay there's something that's going
to happen here
>> was it just the two of you at the time
or
>> yeah No.
>> Transitioning from that to where you are
now, at what point did you know that it
it seems like you knew pretty early on
that this is more than a project, but at
what point were you like, "Okay, this is
for real."
>> Um, so it was always real to me in my my
head, right? I I think I knew from the
beginning that um like this thing can
really become something or it is going
to become a big thing. And um for a
while for us it was a lot about kind of
finding um um finding the way to to
approach it. So I think a lot of people
back then uh for example cursor they you
know you apply it to a GitHub copilot as
well right you apply to improved
developers which is like this very
iterative very logical thing to do um
and I was never really that interested
in in in doing that and I more wanted to
um you know enable people to go 0 to one
as fully nontechnical and I would say
that that's the harder problem
>> and we were a bit early on that. Um so I
think we we had some doubt like they're
in the early days um of will this
actually work? Is the foundational
models good enough for this use case?
And they weren't really uh but you could
do basic prototypes. Um and then I think
in in November now just about over eight
months ago, right, is when we
launched Lovable and that's when things
really started to to take off. So then
it became real not just in my head.
>> Was that when you actually changed the
name to lovable from GPT engineering
engineer which
>> uh is quite catchy I must say but
lovable sounds about how did you how did
you end up on lovable as the name
>> actually changing the name was a big
internal debate I could imagine.
>> Yeah and I was actually a proponent for
keeping Duty engineer for a long time. I
liked it and you kind of get attached to
names but the company name was actually
lovable since it's
>> Oh, I see. So company name was Lovable.
So it just took on the name.
>> Yeah. So we we had this idea of like um
first off I think level is just like a a
nice name but it it also like comes with
this idea of that um you know before in
in kind of how you're building software
you you do some designs maybe low
fidelity then higher fidelity and uh you
build this MVP minimum viable product um
and uh then you you know you build you
throw that away and you build the real
thing and we were thinking like all
right you apply AI to is now you can
just build the real thing and we can
call that the minimum lovable product.
>> Yeah.
>> And therefore lovable.
>> Anything you in the early days you feel
like you should have done different like
any learnings for founders who are
listening uh that you think you could
have done differently that would have
set you up better.
>> One thing would actually be launching
earlier. Um, so we were very early in
the space and I think maybe you know
maybe the first I I at least didn't know
of any other people kind of building
this type of chat and and preview
interface uh um before before we did it.
Um but we weren't the first to go like
and do a general launch of it. And um I
think that's that was a mistake in in
hindsight. We were basically behind a
wait list for for too long. I do
remember Ivan and Simon uh founders of
Notion uh basically a year ago saying to
me, "You got to check out Lovable and
like going on there and checking it
out." So like whatever you did actually
worked and people took notice for sure.
>> Yeah.
>> Uh I'm curious as you started scaling
and obviously you know from 1 million to
100 million it takes a different type of
leadership. You mentioned how you're
here for a long time. Y
>> uh all of a sudden you have to hire
chiefs of this, chiefs of that. Like how
do you actually stay hands-on? Uh you
mentioned you wake up 4 in the morning
while you're out here. Tell me more
about how you run the business and how
you're able to like uh on a day-to-day
basis. How are you able to run the
business? How do you not become a
meeting CTO, if you will?
>> Yeah. Um first of I try to say no to all
meetings and then people have to
convince me to actually join the
meeting. So that's that's a great way.
Let me take a note for them. I don't do
that here.
>> And also when you actually do meeting,
try to keep them very short. I've heard
Ivan is a big backer of that.
>> Ivan, I can't sit down for with him for
dinner more than half an hour. He he he
wants to eat. I love [laughter]
>> um Yeah. So I mean for me I I love
building product and you know I think
even this might be a thing for I think a
lot of kind of technical founders that
you start very close to the product and
the engineering and then you have to um
kind of step back a bit at least from um
the very um like ground level things um
and that has definitely been a bit of a
transition but I'm I'm still very much
involved with the day-to-day on uh you
know both high and low
But it's you got to choose a bit more
where where to spend the time.
>> And I'm curious, how do you divide your
time between you and Anton? He you
mentioned how he was a CTO of his
company as well. Obviously,
[clears throat] he's very technical.
Yeah.
>> And like how do you decide uh who does
what and like not step on each other's?
>> Um so we we kind of just looked at the
workshart and said like all right, hey,
all right, I'll take kind of
engineering, product, and design and
you'll take the rest. Um, and I had
someone ask me when I told them this
like, "Oh, so you're the technical one."
I'm like, "No, we're we're both kind of
technical and I think we we kind of
overlap a bit and look on on both sides
as well." Uh, so the split isn't isn't
that clear, but that's the the general
uh direction of us.
>> Very cool. And it it would you say your
uh basically your engineering or is flat
or like how do you how do you manage the
team? Like do you have a lot of
managers? had I mean 70 people or so
>> in the company.
>> Yeah. Yeah. Exactly. So um yeah, I mean
the the base idea in how I want to build
a company and especially engineering as
well is to keep it keep it very flat and
uh just have this uh kind of ideas
floating around and let the best idea
win and and do that thing
and I think that's something
we're you know at the size so we're
around like 40 engineers and I think
that still works really well for us,
And then I'm curious as you're scaling
uh you know what are the um you know
you're reinventing basically how people
develop software
>> uh through natural language. Um what are
the processes that like traditional
companies use like OKRs and like roadmap
and things that you're like we don't
need that. What are the things that
you've actually adopted? M um well I do
think you kind of use a lot of the um
things that have been defined before you
but I think this whole new technology
with with foundational models uh it it
changes the rules um and I feel like
we're definitely part of like figuring
out okay what what are the new rules and
uh it's often about um you know just
these base ideas of oh hey maybe you
don't need to be this many people or um
maybe you know writing a product uh
requirement document you don't have to
do that because actually just uh
building the prototype uh you can do
that very cheaply and very quickly.
>> Yeah. One of the things that I love is
that I work on a product that I use
every day and all day and I get to like
basically uh dog food it and try it out
before our customers do. How do you do
that at uh lovable? Do you ask the team
to actually create their own apps? Like
tell me more about like is there a dog
food process? Uh do you are you
constantly building new apps on lovable?
>> Yes. Yes, very much so. And it's almost
a problem because it's a it's a bit
addicting when you when you get started.
I um I think right before the flight
over here to San Francisco, I um I
stayed up a bit too late one night
because I was like building this office
CRM in in Lovable. And uh so that's a a
a really big thing for us to be kind of
the first customer of of our product.
And a lot that we use it for is kind of
these internal tooling. So we don't have
any traditional kind of ERP uh type
software. We just we build it with
lovable.
>> That's very cool. And what uh as you're
you know I'm sure you uh you're you see
a lot of products that are built on
lovable.
>> Uh what are some that you feel like oh
my god I cannot believe someone built
that unlovable? M
>> any that any of your favorites or
complex ones?
>> Yeah, I mean a lot of the interesting
ones are actually quite simple like the
ones that go viral. So there was one
that like takes some picture and does
video editing on it locally in your
browser and that went like absolutely
viral. Um, and I was uh at one of our
colleagues like birthday party the other
day and then a guy comes up to me and he
starts talking about this um very like
intricate like SAS that he's building
and I thought it was just building it
for like the company he was working at.
It was uh um around like figuring out
based on how much square footage like
how much floor tiling and everything you
need like a construction business. But
no, he was actually like doing this as a
side project, like taking the things he
was learning and like selling these
misses and and making, you know, quite
decent money on it. So I think that's a
really cool thing that's that's
happening with a lot of people,
especially with some industry knowledge
kind of just applying that now and all
of a sudden they can just do it
themselves.
>> Very cool. Do you see a world uh maybe a
year or two years down the line where
someone will build Lovable off of
Lovable? I know that sounds weird, like
you have little lovable children.
>> Yeah, I mean I do that every now and
then. [laughter]
I do that definitely. And yeah, I would
say it's just a matter of time before um
like a lot of the things we're building
now become trivial to build.
>> Yeah. No,
>> very cool. And uh I I one of the quotes
that I uh read about and it's uh you
said you hit your head against the wall
until it worked.
>> Can you tell us more about that? Like
what what were you working on and what
exactly were you referring to? So that
was really about um the beginning of um
we were choosing and debating me and
Anton a lot on like okay what's our
what's our customer and uh what's our
product and u I would say on the on the
product side of things we were very much
thinking about hey should we do
something like in one vertical maybe
only build landing pages or only build
landing pages for restaurants or these
type of ideas right or we do uh what we
ended up doing which is uh just build
something very broad and uh I'm I'm very
happy we did the ladder of building
something broad
>> uh as your like uh level uh is doing
more of like using natural language to
produce all these like softwares I'm
curious what does production ready mean
to you? Yeah. So I think especially
early on and this is actively changing
but AI is really good at creating new
things. Um and the thing with software
is that um the the more complex and the
larger the code base becomes the harder
is to to change and like the complexity
of uh of adding or even just maintaining
it increases. So it's a very like
central thing that we're um um focused
on on just this like okay once you've
created it um how can you keep on adding
to it and keep on improving it uh and um
taking it to production is you know the
first version is quite easy but then
like you have a database and you need to
do migrations on top of that and I think
that's a factor of kind of where
foundational models are today but um I
think a lot of people are kind of
underestimating even what it what it can
do today especially if you've coding for
a long time and you're like, "Oh, no. I
have to do this myself." Like, do you
really like try try doing it first by
taking a step back?
>> And do you see a world where I know uh
you mentioned earlier how you're really
targeting the 99% who don't code as
much,
>> but do you see yourself like moving
either those people who don't know how
to code, they become better coders, like
pick up stuff, or do you gravitate more
towards like people who can code? So the
the idea with lovable is really to
abstract these technical choices so that
you maybe like the example I gave before
of someone working in construction like
they should be thinking about
construction and choosing a choosing a
database and like choosing underlying
tech stack is something I think lovable
should handle for you. uh and um also
something that we're actively kind of
making sure that we encode uh in into
the AI um to make sure that it actually
does uh good technical choices from the
start.
>> So eventually people don't have to worry
about the signing up for superbase or
something like that. You're going to
take care of that for them.
>> Exactly. Exactly. Do you know our road
map? Like have you
>> I watch your videos.
>> Yes. Yeah. [laughter]
>> Yeah. Or you have very good intuition.
Yeah. That's very cool. I I actually
love that.
>> Give us a preview of like how you're
working with the you mentioned how you
uh were working with the notion team on
like with MCP server. Uh what should
builders expect uh to come? So I think
lovable plus notion is a very
interesting combo because notion is the
place where a lot of people spend a lot
of their time and kind of dumping
context and then lovable is this place
where you can create
software and create apps and a lot of
the time the limiting factor to do that
is the context and that's true for a lot
of different AI tooling. So, notion and
lovablely is a very match made made in
heaven in that sense that you can
actually just leverage all of this
context to start creating stuff.
>> Very cool. Well, I look forward to uh
the partnership and uh see what the
teams do together.
Has it become harder to like uh than 5
years ago, 10 years ago to build a
startup? Uh is it harder, easier? I do
think it's actually a great time to
create startups and you know that's core
of like our business idea as well that
hey you don't actually need to know all
of these like technical skills that
could take you years to to acquire and
learn but you could just kind of have
this idea or or have this kind of
unrelated skill set to software and and
even do like a software startup and then
I think us is also like an example of
hey you can actually kind of leverage
the the power of the the foundational
models and and do something that creates
a lot of value.
>> All right, we'll close where we always
do uh with Trust Block uh with advice.
>> Okay, and I'm going to ask you two
questions.
>> Imagine you're speaking directly to the
next generation of founders. What's one
building block of advice you'd leave
behind for early stage founders? One
thing that I think we did incorrectly at
Lovable at part of our journey at least
is to try to build a bit too much for
the future. So we were building this
very complex agent before agents really
worked and uh we were like oh it's going
to work with GP5 don't worry. Um and
that was a big mistake. uh and in
hindsight I think really just looking at
like okay hey this is the value that we
can create today with uh with
foundational models there's um there's a
lot on the table still uh in in a lot of
different verticals um so I would really
say to to a founder today like hey just
figure out what you can do with these
models and what use cases you can serve
today don't think too much about the
future like we have a steam engine here
ready to go just apply it
>> I I love that There's a lot of emphasis
in uh this in all the talk that you've
done about speed and like moving as fast
as possible and also meeting with uh
satisfying use cases. I love that.
>> Yeah.
>> Um the second question and my last
question, if you had to rebuild Lovable
from scratch today, what's the first
block you'd start with?
>> Oh, well that's easy. I would just use
Lovable.
>> Okay, [laughter] great.
